coredata.dc:title: Designing a sustainable fluorescent targeting probe for superselective nucleus imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Liang
Li, Ming
Li, Yongle
Wu, Bin
Chen, Hailong
Wang, Ruining
Xu, Tao
Guo, Huazhang
Li, Weitao
Joyner, Jarin
Shen, Xingcan
Le, Mengying
Vajtai, Robert
Ajayan, Pulickel M.
Ren, Wei
Wu, Minghong
coredata.dc:description: Specific targeting of cell organelles is indispensable for bioimaging and diagnostics but remains a challenge due to the limitation of highly selective fluorescent targeting probes. Herein, we successfully designed a molecular fusion route to controllably synthesize bright orange fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The relative nitrogen doping level of the GQDs reached 18.88 at%. The GQDs exhibited long-wavelength excitation fluorescence and photoluminescence (PL) stability. Femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy elucidated that the sp2 cluster and the surface state's synergistic effect contributed to the PL. Furthermore, the most alluring discovery was that the GQDs had good biocompatibility and an unprecedented targeting capability for superselective nucleus imaging without staining cell organelles. Molecular dynamics simulations revealed that once the GQDs were transported across the plasma membrane, the higher the N-doping ratio of the GQDs was, and the easier the GQDs penetrated the membrane. The reason may stem from the compatibility of –NH2 with lipid molecules, which is higher than that of –OH. Enriched nitrogen doping in GQDs is beneficial for crossing the cell membrane to the target nucleus. Our findings could provide scientific theory and a technical basis to design nucleus-targeting probes in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen-doping
Nucleus-targeting imaging
Fluorescence probes
Molecular dynamic simulations

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectrum of GQDs. (b) High-resolution N1s spectrum of GQDs. (c) 1H NMR spectrum of GQDs. (d) 13C NMR spectrum of GQDs. (e) AFM image and height distribution of GQDs. (f) TEM image and lateral size distribution of GQDs. (g) High-resolution TEM image and FFT pattern of GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Optical spectra of UV–visible absorption (ABS), PL, and PL excitation (PLE). (Inset: a fluorescence photograph of GQD solution under a UV light). (b) PL spectra of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Semi-log curve for time-resolved PL spectrum of GQDs. (d) Two-dimensional pseudocolor map of the transient absorption (TA) spectra of GQDs expressed in ΔmOD (the change of the absorption intensity of the sample after excitation) as a function of both delay time and probe wavelength excited at 400 nm. (e) TA spectra of GQDs at indicated delay times from 0.45 ps to 1000 ps. (f) The decay tendency of GQDs at different wavelengths. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Confocal fluorescent images of living Hela cells contained with DAPI excited at 405 nm (a) and GQDs excited at 488 nm (b), 514 nm (c), 543 nm (d), merged image (e). (f) Fluorescence intensity analysis of Hela cells co-incubation with DAPI at 405 nm as the control and GQDs at 488, 514 and 543 nm by ImageJ. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) GQDs through the cell biofilm membrane by MD simulations. (b) The force varying with time during GQDs going through the membrane. (c) The corresponding free energy varying with time during GQDs going through the membrane. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Designing a sustainable fluorescent targeting probe for superselective nucleus imaging

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Specific targeting of cell organelles is indispensable for bioimaging and diagnostics but remains a challenge due to the limitation of highly selective fluorescent targeting probes. Herein, we successfully designed a molecular fusion route to controllably synthesize bright orange fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The relative nitrogen doping level of the GQDs reached 18.88 at%. The GQDs exhibited long-wavelength excitation fluorescence and photoluminescence (PL) stability. Femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy elucidated that the sp2 cluster and the surface state's synergistic effect contributed to the PL. Furthermore, the most alluring discovery was that the GQDs had good biocompatibility and an unprecedented targeting capability for superselective nucleus imaging without staining cell organelles. Molecular dynamics simulations revealed that once the GQDs were transported across the plasma membrane, the higher the N-doping ratio of the GQDs was, and the easier the GQDs penetrated the membrane. The reason may stem from the compatibility of –NH2 with lipid molecules, which is higher than that of –OH. Enriched nitrogen doping in GQDs is beneficial for crossing the cell membrane to the target nucleus. Our findings could provide scientific theory and a technical basis to design nucleus-targeting probes in the future.

Enriched nitrogen-doping in graphene quantum dots enables a high-selective fluorescent probe for nucleus-targeting imaging.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The cellular nucleus, the largest and most prominent organelle in cells, is the cell's regulatory center for genetics and metabolism. Understanding its activity pace plays a vital role in the constitution of efficient measurements for cancer diagnostics and treatment. Previously reported works elucidated that the ligand-receptor binding interaction is an active pathway for endowing a multitude of fluorescent probes targeting nuclear features [1–6]. Multilayer targeting bioconjugates have been constructed by combining gold nanoparticles [3,4] and CdSe/ZnS quantum dots [5,6] with targeting ligands (nuclear antibodies and nucleus-localization peptides) to strengthen receptor-mediated endocytosis by cancer cells. Although various fluorescent probes have achieved recognition effects for nuclear imaging, conventional ligand-mediated targeting strategies suffer from a myriad of biological impediments that hinder their clinical applications, including inefficient nucleus targeting, systemic safety concerns, and poor scalability in complex construction [1–6].

Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new member of the carbon nanomaterial family, have attracted extensive attention due to their superior optical performance, eco-friendliness, sustainability, and biocompatibility. Due to these properties, GQDs have multiple potential applications, such as in white LEDs [7], photo(electro)catalysis [8–10], bioimaging [11,12], and biomedicine [13–16]. Although previous reports demonstrated that GQDs could enter a cell nucleus, they also exposed some shortcomings [17–23]. Similar to conventional ligand-mediated targeting nanomaterials, the great majority of GQDs target the nucleus in conjunction with nucleus-localized biological reagents, making the manufacturing process complicated and expensive [17–19]. Furthermore, the boundary between the nucleus and other organelles is not accurately determined in the imaging process. The imaging range often covers the entire cell field, but more GQDs are located in the nucleus [20–23]. Therefore, the targeting imaging of GQDs did not represent a particular nucleus selectivity. More importantly, the transmembrane mechanism of GQDs is unclear. Recently, our group fabricated edge-chlorinated and aminated GQDs. Compared to other previously reported GQDs, the GQDs displayed higher selective imaging of the nucleus, but a few GQDs were retained in the cytoplasm [24]. This discovery illustrated that the development of practical approaches for synthesizing GQDs is a vital pathway for addressing nucleus-targeting imaging selectivity.

To overcome the limitations of current GQDs as nucleus-targeting probes, we designed a modified molecular fusion route to synthesize high-quality fluorescent GQDs on a large scale. The N content of the GQDs reached 18.88 at%. The GQDs exhibit long-wavelength excitation fluorescent properties and emit bright orange light under ultraviolet (UV)-irradiation. The luminescence mechanism and the carrier relaxation dynamics of GQDs were further analyzed by femtosecond transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy. Furthermore, reliable targeting strategies were established for the use of GQDs in cell imaging and the transmembrane mechanism of GQDs was simulated by molecular dynamics (MD). High-level nitrogen doping of GQDs is beneficial for crossing the cell membrane to the target nucleus. These findings provide an opportunity to design carbon-based fluorescent nucleus-targeting probes for cancer diagnosis and therapy in the near future.

1,2,4-triaminobenzene (50 mg) and NH3OH (0.5 mL) were dissolved in DI water (10 mL), and the solution was then transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained solution was dialyzed in a 3.5 K Da dialysis bag to remove the unreacted precursor. The DI water solution outside of the dialysis bag needed to change every 6–8 h and sustained the dialyzed process one week for guaranteeing to wholly remove the precursor. Then, the solution in the dialysis bag was dried at 80 °C for structural characterization. For optical and cell experiments, the resultant GQDs were directly used without purification.

AFM images were taken using an SPM-9600 AFM. TEM observations were performed on aberration-corrected TEM (FEI Titan 80–300) operating at an 80 kV acceleration voltage and a charge-coupled device camera (2k × 2k, Gatan UltraScan 1000) was used for image recording with an exposure time of 1–2 s. XRD patterns were obtained with a Rigaku 18 KW D/max-2550 using Cu Kα radiation. Absorption and fluorescence spectra were registered at room temperature on a Hitachi 3100 spectrophotometer and a Hitachi 7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. FT-IR spectra of dried samples were recognized with a Bio-Rad FTIR spectrometer FTS165. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer with λex = 633 nm. XPS spectra were gathered using a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Zeta potentials of GQDs were measured at room temperature using a zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, Zetasizer Nano, ZS90). The photographs were taken with a camera (Nikon, D7000) under UV light excited at 365 nm. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectroscopy were carried out with JEOL resonance ECZ400S 400 MHz spectrometer using DMSO-D6 as the solvent.

A regeneratively amplified Ti: sapphire laser system (Spitfire Ace, Spectra-Physics) provides the fundamental light source. The pump pulse (400 nm) is generated by focusing a portion of fundamental light into BBO crystal. The other fundamental pulse provides broadband probe pulse (white light continuum) that is produced by focusing 800 nm fundamental light into a 3 mm sapphire plate. A motorized delay stage is used to control the time delay between the pump and probe beams, both of which overlap in the sample with crossing areas of 200 μm and 50 μm, respectively. After passing through the sample, the probe pulse is focused into an optical fiber that is coupled to the spectrometer (AvaSpec-ULS2048CL-EVO, Avantes). The energy of 400 nm excitation pulse is adjusted to about 50 nJ/pulse by a neutral density optical filter. The pump pulse is chopped at 500 Hz to acquire pumped and un-pumped probe spectra, and the ΔOD spectrum can be obtained by processing them. The solutions are placed in a 2 mm optical path length quartz cuvette. Both the instrument response function (150 fs) and temporal chirp in the probe light are determined by measuring the cross-modulation of solvent, and the group velocity dispersion effect on the experiment data is corrected accordingly.

Hela cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle (DMEM) medium (Gibco, USA) with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (Hyclone, USA). Approximately 2 × 105 Hela cells were seeded in culture dishes (diameter: 35 mm dish with 14 mm bottom well) and cultured using the same culture medium (2 mL per dish) at 37 °C under 5% CO2/95% air. All cells were incubated for at least 24 h until ∼80% confluence was reached. The GQDs aqueous solution was introduced into the cells at a final concentration of ∼5 mg/L in the culture medium. After 5 min of incubation, Hela cells were then washed twice with PBS to remove excessive GQDs. The cells were examined under a confocal microscope (Leica TCS SP5) using lasers of 405, 488, 514, and 543 nm. The optical absorption range of the mixture at 405, 488, 514, and 543 nm excitation was measured at 420–480, 490–510, 520–540, and 555–600 nm, respectively.

A GQDs aqueous solution was introduced into the cells at a final concentration of ∼5 mg/L in the culture medium. For illustrating whether the organelle entered by the GQDs is indeed the cell nucleus, the Hela cells were cultured for 20 min and washed two times with PBS to remove excessive GQDs. Moreover. The cells were washed once with 0.5 mL DAPI dye (Keygen Institute of Biotechnology, China) with a concentration of 2 μg/mL. Then 0.5 mL DAPI dye was added and incubated at 37 °C for 15 min. After 15 min of incubation, Hela cells were then washed three times with PBS to remove the dye. The cells were reviewed by a confocal microscope (Leica TCS SP5) for further observation.

Cell viability was performed using an MTT assay with 3-(4,5-dimethyl-thiazole-2-yl)-2,5-phenyltetrazolium bromide. The cell viability was measured as the percentage of viable cells in total cells. Hela cells were seeded in 96-well plates (5 × 103 cells per well). After pre-culturing for 24 h, the medium was replaced by a fresh one containing the GQDs at a dose of 5, 10, 20, and 40 mg/L for 6, 12, and 24 h. In each well, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added and then incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. 150 μL DMSO was substituted for the medium, and the absorbance of each well at 490 nm was measured using a Microplate Reader (Thermo, USA).
The cellular uptake mechanisms of GQDs were performed by blocking uptake pathways with different treatments. The experimental details are similar to our previous work [24].

To simulate the bacterial membrane, a model consisting of zwitterionic lipid dioleoylphosphatidylethanolamine (DOPE) and lipid 1,2-Dioleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (DOPC) have been used in this work. The lipid bilayer mixture of DOPE and DOPG was generated by CHARMM-GUI [25], which were solvated by water molecules in a box of 109 × 109 × 135 Å [26]. Na+ neutralized the negative charges of the lipids in the water layers. There is a total of 165979 atoms in the unit cell. The simulation system and the corresponding force field parameters were adopted from the previous simulation of an oligomer interacting with a membrane,3 except those of GQDs (see below), and the water molecules are re-added to maintain proper accommodation of the GQDs.

The GQDs contains 375C, 24 N, 25 O, and 73H atoms separately, and is set to be neutral. The N is in the –NH2 form and the O is in the –OH form. The force field parameters for it are the same as those used in previous studies [27], except the –NH2 group adopted from the corresponding group on 3-aminopyridine in Generalized CHARMM force field [28].

The membrane system was first minimized for 20000 steps with all non-hydrogen atoms were fixed, then for another 20000 steps with only the phosphorus atoms harmonically constrained (k = 5 kcal mol−1 Å−2). After minimization, 20 ps MD equilibration was performed with the constraints in place, then by 4 ns equilibration with k scaled by 2, 1, 0.5, and 0 every 1 ns. The time step was 1 fs during all simulations with the SHAKE [29] algorithm. The periodical boundary conditions (PBCs) were enforced in our simulations. All the membrane-related systems were in the NPT ensemble at T = 310 K to ensure the membrane in the liquid phase. The pressure was set to 1 atm. To maintain constant temperature and pressure, stochastic Langevin dynamics [30] and Nose–Hoover Langevin piston pressure [31] control was used. The SHAKE algorithm was used for all covalent bonds involving hydrogen.

After the relaxation step, a steered MD [32] with the standard protocol was performed by adding a gentle force at the center of the mass of the GQDs, to assist in overcoming the potential barrier of the membrane and letting us observe its interaction with the membrane during the membrane-penetrating process. The penetrating process was successfully observed within 1 ns. After that, the GQDs has passed the membrane and get to the opposite side of the membrane.

Using the controllable modified molecular fusion route, highly fluorescent GQDs were facilely fabricated by hydrothermally treating 1,2,4-triaminobenzene and ammonia in a water solution (Fig. S1). The prepared GQDs can be dispersed in water to emit bright orange fluorescence under UV light (inset in Fig. 2a). The Fourier-transform IR (FTIR) spectrum of the GQDs (Fig. 1 a) displays abundant amino (NH2) and hydroxyl (OH) groups on their surfaces. Such groups include O–H (3407 cm−1), N–H (3209 cm−1), CO (1633 cm−1), CC (1473 cm−1), C–N (1309 cm−1), and C–OH (1091 cm−1) groups [33]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis shows that the GQDs comprised C, N, and O atoms (Fig. S2a). The N and O contents of the GQDs reached 18.88 at% and 10.81 at%, respectively, revealing that there were a greater number of nitrogen containing groups than oxygen containing groups on the surface of the GQDs. The results of the elemental analysis also illustrated that the N content of the GQDs exceeded 18.2 at% (Table S1). In the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 1b, Figs. S2b and c, and Table S2), the N1s band contained peaks at 399.34 and 400.71 eV for amino N and pyrrolic N, respectively [34]. The C1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into three peak corresponding to CC (284.8 eV), C–N (285.88 eV), and C–O (287.43 eV). The O1s band indicated the presence of O–H (532.22 eV) and O–C (533.26 eV). Moreover, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy (1H and 13C) was employed to distinguish the functional groups on the GQDs [35,36] (Fig. 1c and d). In the 1H NMR spectrum, in addition to the apparent aromatic hydrogen signals (7–8 ppm), active hydrogen signals from OH groups with one peak at 5.97 ppm, as shown in Fig. 1c, were also observed. In addition, active H signals from NH2 groups with broad peaks in the ranges of 2.7–4.3 and 6–7 ppm were also present. The 13C NMR spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 1d) further indicates the functional groups of GQDs. In the 13C NMR spectrum, the observed resonance signals in the range of 100–150 ppm correspond to sp2 C atoms, which further illustrates the construction of sp2 clusters during the synthesis procedure of the GQDs. Overall, the GQDs are large-sized conjugated sp2 clusters with an abundance of OH and NH2 groups on their surfaces, which is in agreement with the FTIR and XPS results. Because there was a larger quantity of NH2 groups than OH groups on the surface of GQDs, the GQDs exhibit a slight positive charge at a near-neutral pH (a positive zeta potential of 3–12 mV for pH 6.6–7.4) (Fig. S3).

The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of GQDs demonstrates their heights ranging from 0.9 to 2.24 nm (Fig. 1e), showing that the GQDs mainly comprise 2–5 graphene layers. The GQDs are nearly monodispersed at an average size of 4.41 nm, as shown in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1f). The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1g and Fig. S4) illustrates the high crystallinity of GQDs. The lattice spacing of GQDs is 0.21 nm (inset in Fig. 1g), which confirms the (100) lattice spacing of graphene [36]. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern indicates a (002) peak located at 26.5° that corresponds to the graphitic structure of the GQDs (Fig. S5) [37]. In addition, Raman spectroscopy was employed to determine the high graphitization of the GQDs (Fig. S6). The G-band peak at 1524 cm−1 and the D-band peak at 1385 cm−1 are remarkably observed. The intensity ratios of the D-to G-bands for GQDs were approximately 1.1. We estimated that one molecule of 1,2,4-triaminobenzene consisting of one benzene ring similar to the basic building blocks of graphene, can work as a great building block for the preparation of high-crystallinity GQDs via cyclodeamination and polycondensation reactions, as demonstrated in Fig. S1.

To gain more insight into the optical characteristics of the GQDs, UV/Vis spectroscopy was performed. As shown in Fig. 2 a, there is an obvious excitonic absorption band at approximately 580 nm in the UV–vis spectrum, indicating the high conjugation of the GQDs. Two absorption maxima at 270 and 432 nm in the UV/Vis absorption spectrum of GQDs were assigned to the π→π∗ transition of the aromatic CC bond, and the n→π∗ transitions of the aromatic sp2 system containing CO and CN bonds (Fig. 2a) respectively [38]. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of GQDs are observed at 450 nm and 587 nm, respectively, and show a bright orange color under UV light (inset in Fig. 2a). Notably, excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) was observed (Fig. 2b), and the emission peaks were primarily present at ∼590 nm within the excitation wavelength range from 400 to 580 nm, which is beneficial for efficient observation of cell imaging under different excitation wavelengths and more advantageous than that of other majority fluorescent carbon materials[18–21]. In the control experiment, we prepared control GQDs without adding NH3OH (WA-GQDs) under the same reaction conditions. Unlike that of the GQDs, the weak absorption peak of the WA-GQDs was located at 315 nm (Fig. S7a), which is more blueshifted than that of the GQDs (432 nm). The PL and PLE wavelengths of the WA-GQDs were 453 and 365 nm, respectively, which correspond to their blue fluorescent color. In contrast, the WA-GQDs exhibited excitation-dependent PL behavior, clarifying the highly crystalline structure of the GQDs that occurs when NH3OH is included in the synthesis process. Furthermore, the PL decay (Fig. 2c and Fig. S8) was a single exponential with a 2.6 ns lifetime. Moreover, the PL stability of the prepared GQDs at different pH values in the cell microenvironment was also measured (Fig. S9). The PL intensity slightly increased with increasing pH ranging from 6.6 to 7.4, whereas the UV–visible absorption of GQDs did not change. When GQDs enter the cell solution, slight pH changes in the cell microenvironment do not affect the optical properties of the GQDs. In addition, we calculated the stability of the PL intensity of the GQDs at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. S10, we discovered that the PL intensity of the GQDs increased with increasing temperature, exhibiting the temperature sensing performance of the GQDs.

To further scrutinize the carrier relaxation dynamics and the luminescence mechanism of GQDs, TA spectroscopy measurements were performed [39,40] (Fig. 2d). The TA spectra of the GQDs at 400 nm excitation are displayed in Fig. 2d with the probe in the 450–800 nm range and the scan delay time from 0.1 ps to 1 ns. The positive (red) features at 500 nm belong to the excited state absorption (ESA), and the relatively negative (blue) features from 550 to 750 nm correspond to the stimulated emission (SE) of the PL spectrum. The TA spectra of the GQDs with different delay times and the decay tendency of GQDs at different wavelengths are shown in Fig. 2e and f, respectively. The negative SE peaks located at 610 nm increase within 10 ps by degrees. Global analysis was used to determine the detailed relaxation channels of the excited carriers from the TA data, and four exponential decay functions were fitted. The four fitted time constants were 0.26 ps, 2 ps, 75 ps, and 2.6 ns. The fitted decay associated difference spectra (DADS) and decay dynamics of GQDs are shown in Figs. S11 and S12. It can be observed that, the SE signal increases within the first lifetime to the second lifetime, but decays in the third and fourth lifetimes. Furthermore, the percentages of the decay dynamics are evaluated by the four decay channels of different wavelengths (Fig. S13), which are analyzed as follows: 1) the percentage of the first component is zero at approximately 560 nm; 2) the proportion of both the third and fourth units is zero at approximately 575 nm; 3) the rate of the fourth part is zero at 695 nm; and 4) the portion of the third component near 725 nm is zero. Guided by the above analysis, the four parts are attributed to the corresponding relaxation channels. The coulomb-induced hot carriers in the sp2 cluster release their excess energy into the surrounding environment after excitation via electron-optical-phonon (electron-electron) scattering within 0.26 ps [41]; the peaks at 500 nm in the ESA spectra increased in a lifetime (Fig. 2e). Some of the relaxed carriers are captured by the surface states that make up the surface groups and carbon skeleton for 2 ps [42], followed by the PL emission via recombination of electrons and holes, and the nonradiative transition into the ground state of the remaining carriers appears. Two recombination time constants of 75 ps and 2.6 ns are observed. Intriguingly, the relatively slower recombination rate of electrons and holes is in agreement with the measured single-exponential lifetime in time-resolved PL spectra, directly demonstrating the same radiative recombination process. Then, the other process of 75 ps arises from nonradiative recombination. The TA results reveal that the synergistic effect of the sp2 cluster and the surface state contributes to the PL [43,44].

The bright orange fluorescent GQDs are promising for applications in cell imaging, as shown in Fig. 3 . Hela cancer cells were used as a model to investigate the nucleus-targeting ability of the GQDs. Confocal imaging demonstrated that all Hela nuclei were brightly stained by the GQDs excited at 488, 514 and 543 nm, indicating efficient nucleus-targeting imaging (Fig. 3b–3d). To illustrate that the region stained by GQDs was the cell nucleus, the Hela cells were co-stained with DAPI dye, which is a commercial nuclear staining dye. With the addition of DAPI dye, the exquisite nuclear architecture was brightly stained under 405 nm excitation (Fig. 3a). We also performed a cell imaging experiment that only used GQDs without DAPI co-staining. As shown in Fig. S14, the Hela cells exhibited fuzzy blue fluorescence under 405 nm excitation. Moreover, the regions stained by the GQDs were identified as cell nuclei by merging the stained images between DAPI and GQDs (Fig. 3e), further confirming the nucleus-targeting behavior. Comparisons of the intracellular fluorescence intensity of the DAPI dye showed that the GQDs exhibited an apparent decrease under 488 nm and 543 nm excitation under the same imaging conditions (Fig. 3f). Surprisingly, the intracellular fluorescence intensity of GQDs excited at 514 nm was stronger than that of the DAPI dye and effectively avoided the interference of short-lived background fluorescence on bioimaging in turn exhibiting robust commercial application prospects.

In addition to the nucleus-targeting capabilities of the GQDs, we also measured their toxicity. Relative tests confirmed the nontoxic nature of the GQDs in Fig. S15. At the concentration of GQDs used in the cell experiments (5 mg/L), there was good biocompatibility (cell survival rate>80%) despite the short culture time (6 h) or long culture time (24 h). When their concentration was increased to 20 mg/L, they exhibited low toxicity, and when the concentration was up to 40 mg/L, unsuitable toxicity was observed. As the concentration used was only 5 mg/L, toxic concentrations were much higher than what is necessary for practical imaging applications. Furthermore, after culturing for 2 h, nucleus-targeting imaging continued to sustain high-quality fluorescent imaging and vigorous intensity (Figs. S16 and S17), exhibiting robust long-term culturing stability for further treating cancer cells.

To explore the transmembrane mechanism of this unique targeting behavior, two methods of adding inhibitors and decreasing the temperature were used in the nucleus-targeting imaging experiments [24]. The experiment is the control group at 37 °C and can block the energy-dependent endocytosis at 4 °C. Meanwhile, the sucrose and Mycostatin inhibit clathrin-mediated and caveolae-mediated pathways, respectively. As shown in Fig. S18c,d and S19, the color of the cells under 514 nm excitation darkened at the addition of Mycostatin and low temperature of 4 °C compared with the control group (37 °C). Meanwhile, pretreatment with sucrose did not specifically hinder the nucleus-targeting effect of GQDs, indicating that this pathway is independent of the clathrin-mediated pathway. Overall, the uptake of GQDs was not only an energy-dependent process but also proceeded by an caveolae-mediated endocytosis mechanisms.

We used pure OH-GQDs [45] as a fluorescent probe to compare imaging targeting capabilities. According to the characterization results above, the gap between the GQDs and OH-GQDs was enriched with N containing –NH2 functional groups on the surface of the GQDs. As shown in Fig. S20, OH-GQDs were only located in the cytoplasmic region, indicating that the OH-GQDs could not cross the nuclear membrane and image the nucleus. Due to the huge gap in the imaging effect between the GQDs and OH-GQDs, we hypothesize that –NH2 plays an essential role in transmembrane crossing.

To verify our hypothesis, we built the system and performed molecular dynamics simulations (MD) [25–32], as shown in Fig. 4 , Fig. S21 and Table S3. Amphiphilic GQDs consist of a hydrophobic graphene core (black) and hydrophilic periphery functionalized by –NH2 and –OH; the cell membrane (white) is modeled as a phospholipid bilayer composed of 192 zwitterionic lipid dioleoylphosphatidylethanolamine (DOPE) and 192 lipid 1,2-dioleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (DOPC), and the water is denoted by the blue bulk. We adjusted the charge of the –OH and –NH2 functional groups according to the electrostatic potential. In the MD simulation, the GQDs were smoothly dragged from one side to the other through the membrane within 1 ns.

To illustrate the interaction between the GQDs and the membrane, we calculated the force and free energy during the constant velocity SMD simulation. According to the results, while the GQDs penetrated the membrane, those with only –NH2 groups at their edge sites required significantly less force than that required for GQDs with –OH or a mix of –OH/-NH2 groups (Fig. 4b and Video S1). When the GQDs had only –OH groups at their edge sites, it appeared that they had the most difficulty in penetrating the membrane. Therefore, we can expect that the higher the N-doping ratio of the GQDs is, the stronger the interaction with the membrane is, and the easier it is for the GQDs to penetrate the membrane. The same conclusion can also be drawn from the results of the free energy experiments shown in Fig. 4c.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2021.04.099

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
1

In summary, we successfully designed a molecular fusion route to controllably synthesize bright orange fluorescent GQDs. Extremely high nitrogen doping (18.88 at%) endows the GQDs with hydrophilic properties. In addition to the long-wavelength excitation fluorescent and excitation-independent PL behaviors, the GQDs exhibit ideal PL stability within the pH range of the cell microenvironment. The femtosecond transient absorption results demonstrate that the sp2 cluster and the surface state synergistically contributed to the PL. Furthermore, the GQDs exhibit an extraordinary targeting ability exactly to the nucleus without constructing sophisticated bioconjugates. Our molecular dynamics simulations suggest that once transported into the membrane, the higher the N-doping ratio of the GQDs is, the easier the GQDs penetrate the membrane; this result is reflected by the compatibility of –NH2 with lipid molecules, which is higher than that of –OH. Our findings could provide theoretical and experimental guidance for designing nucleus-targeting probes in the future.
L. Wang, M. Li, and Y. L. Li contributed equally to this work.

Liang Wang: Supervision, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Ming Li: Visualization, Investigation. Yongle Li: Visualization, Investigation. Bin Wu: Visualization, Investigation. Hailong Chen: Visualization, Investigation. Ruining Wang: Visualization, Investigation. Tao Xu: Visualization, Investigation. Huazhang Guo: Visualization, Investigation. Weitao Li: Visualization, Investigation. Jarin Joyner: Visualization, Investigation. Xingcan Shen: Visualization, Investigation. Mengying Le: Visualization, Investigation. Robert Vajtai: Visualization, Investigation. Pulickel M. Ajayan: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Wei Ren: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Minghong Wu: Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

We thank the Laboratory for Microstructures of Shanghai University and the supercomputing services from AM-HPC.
